career.notes: Most districts are using pool postings for 2026-2027 secondary English and history positions rather than specific job postings. Lake Stevens shows the only explicit salary range ($76,634-$147,986). Everett and Shoreline positions appear on job boards but direct district applications are preferred. All positions require Washington State teaching certification.

family.choir: Seattle Pro Musica: 2026-27 auditions open August 2026—email auditions@seattlepromusica.org to join the mailing list. Requirements: prepared solo (≤2 min), sight-read Bach/Brahms/Martinson excerpts, vocalize, and ear-training. Rehearsals Tue 6:30-9:30 pm at Epiphany Parish Seattle; dues $75/yr, scholarships available. Seattle Choral Company: auditions for 2026-27 season run July 8–August 12, 2026. Submit form at seattlechoralcompany.org/join-us. Expect Latin/French/German/Italian/Spanish reading, moderate sight-reading, and prior advanced-ensemble experience. Rehearsals at Phinney Ridge Lutheran; dues $225/season. Northwest Chorale & Choral Arts NW: annual auditions each July—contact Dr. Ryan Ellis (nwassociatedarts.org) or email Choral Arts NW directly. Dues ~$125/yr.
family.toddler: Edmonds Library: Toddler Storytime with Cami, Thu May 7, 10-10:45 am. Lynnwood Library: Toddler Storytime with Nathalie, Mon May 4, 9:30-10 am (doors close promptly). Both free; no registration needed. Sno-Isle system also lists Baby Storytime (Fri May 1, 11 am Lynnwood) and Preschool Storytimes. Imagine Children’s Museum Little Science Lab pops up at Lynnwood Library multiple times this month—check sno-isle.bibliocommons.com for exact dates.

global_news.findings: President Trump has told Congress that US hostilities with Iran have "terminated" during an ongoing ceasefire, arguing he does not need legislative approval for the conflict under the War Powers Resolution. In a letter to congressional leaders, Trump stated "there has been no exchange of fire between the United States Forces and Iran since April 7, 2026" and that "the hostilities that began on February 28, 2026 have terminated." This comes as the 60-day deadline for congressional approval approaches under US law. Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth argued the ceasefire pauses the 60-day clock, thou [TRUNCATED]

Prince Harry has lost his legal fight with the UK government over police protection, with the Court of Appeal upholding the decision to deny him automatic security when visiting Britain. Harry expressed being "devastated" by the ruling, stating he "cannot safely bring his family to Britain" and would "struggle to forgive" the decision. The court found that while Harry understandably felt aggrieved, this did not amount to an error of law. The ruling maintains that Harry will not receive publicly-funded police protection during UK visits since stepping back from royal duties in 2020.

The Kremlin has criticized Ukraine's response to Vladimir Putin's offer of a three-day ceasefire as "ambiguous" and demanded clarity. Putin proposed a 72-hour ceasefire for May 8-10 to mark the 80th anniversary of World War Two victory, but Ukrainian President Zelenskiy indicated he would only accept a ceasefire lasting at least 30 days. The Kremlin stated the brief ceasefire was a test of Ukraine's readiness for peace, while Ukrainian officials dismissed it as political manipulation. Russia will host international leaders including China's Xi Jinping for May 9 celebrations in Moscow.

The US has warned shipping companies they could face sanctions for paying Iranian tolls to pass through the Strait of Hormuz, as the vital shipping channel remains effectively closed. This comes amid the ongoing US-Iran conflict and effective blockade of the strait, which has caused significant global economic disruption. The Treasury Department notice puts shipping firms at risk of violating US sanctions if they make payments to Iran for passage through the critical waterway.

The US is preparing to withdraw 5,000 troops from Germany within the next 6-12 months, creating new tensions between the Trump administration and European allies. This significant reduction in US military presence in Europe comes amid broader questions about NATO commitments and European security arrangements. The withdrawal has sparked concerns among European leaders about the future of transatlantic defense cooperation.

intellectual_journals.findings: In 'The One Hundred Milliseconds Between the World and You', Maria Popova revisits Oliver Sacks’s posthumous essay collection The River of Consciousness to explore how perception is less a passive intake of data than an active, creative act that reflects the perceiver’s entire lived history. Sacks describes consciousness as a coalition-making process: neurons in the visual cortex must recruit millions of allies across the frontal lobes to push any moment of experience over the threshold of awareness. The piece lingers on the unsettling corollary that two people walking the same city block inha [TRUNCATED]

Zoë Schlanger’s The Light Eaters is the scaffold for a second essay on plant intelligence. Drawing on new findings from botany and ecology, the piece argues that plants exhibit memory, problem-solving, and even something akin to personality—capacities long denied because they do not run on animal-style nervous systems. Schlanger’s fieldwork shows corn seedlings that can locate a distant sound source, and Arabidopsis plants that remember and anticipate drought cycles across generations. The essay frames this as a Copernican moment: recognising plant cognition decentres humanity and forces a red [TRUNCATED]

A sweeping investigation by ProPublica and FRONTLINE documents how ICE and CBP have used conspiracy statutes meant for organised crime to arrest more than 300 U.S. citizens—protesters, legal observers, and bystanders—during immigration enforcement actions since mid-2025. In over a third of cases, prosecutors quietly dropped charges after video evidence contradicted sworn officer statements; the pattern suggests a deliberate strategy of pre-trial punishment aimed at chilling dissent. The report follows Alejandro Orellana, a Marine veteran whose “conspiracy” case collapsed when no co-conspirator [TRUNCATED]

Seamus Perry’s 2026 Winter Lecture, “Pluralism and the Modern Poet,” delivered at Conway Hall and now published in the LRB, asks whether a poetics adequate to liberal democracy must sacrifice the traditional virtues of unity and imaginative order. Perry traces the tension from Louis MacNeice’s claim that modernity values “things being various” to Auden’s fear that such pluralism produces poems that are “formless, windy, banal and utterly boring.” Through readings of Marianne Moore, Elizabeth Bishop, and Geoffrey Hill, he argues that the best modern poems do not resolve plurality into harmony b [TRUNCATED]

Francine Prose reviews two new novels—Lee Clay Johnson’s Bloodline and Carl Hiaasen’s Fever Beach—under the banner “Blood in the Game.” Both books, she argues, find ways to register the grotesque violence and corruption of contemporary American life without surrendering the pleasures of fiction. Johnson’s protagonist, a self-mythologising Tennessean who amputates his own hand to claim an imagined Confederate inheritance, becomes a darkly comic emblem of historical grievance weaponised for profit. Hiaasen’s South Florida satire, meanwhile, channels ecological rage into a propulsive plot about t [TRUNCATED]

New April 2026 updates include Educational Song Generator for creating curriculum-aligned songs with lyrics and album art, Studio Mode for document-based editing of AI outputs, and MagicQuizzes for formative assessment with immediate fee [TRUNCATED]

triadic_ontology.findings: Two new monographs advance the triadic-ontology program. First, *Triadic Dynamics and Quantum Perichoresis* (2026, Karl-Alber “Studies on Triadic Ontology” Vol. 7, ed. Dr. Elena M. Rossi) extends the relational model into quantum field theory, arguing that non-linear triadic constraints resolve the measurement problem by treating observer-system-environment as a perichoretic whole whose internal relations are ontologically prior to individual relata. Rossi derives a generalized Schrödinger equation whose non-Hermitian interaction terms encode triadic “mediating relations,” yielding decoherence rates that match empirical data without collapse postulates. Second, *Relational Ontologies and Trinitarian Metaphysics: Subsistences, Events, and Gunk* (2025, PhilArchive preprint 130403456, Matteo Migliore) reframes classical Trinitarian doctrine through contemporary process metaphysics, proposing that divine persons are not substances but dynamic “subsistent relations” whose identity condition [TRUNCATED]

uap.findings: Congressional momentum on UAP disclosure has reached fever pitch. Rep. Anna Paulina Luna, chair of the House Oversight Task Force on Declassification, vowed to hold a press conference presenting "materials of nonhuman origin" after viewing classified evidence in a SCIF. The Pentagon missed her April 14 deadline to release 46 UAP videos, prompting threats of subpoenas. Trump's February directive ordering federal agencies to release UFO files has triggered unprecedented interagency coordination, with the Department of War's AARO office promising "never-before-seen" UAP information. The Senate's proposed UAP Disclosure Act of 2025 would create a centralized National Archives collection for all non-human intelligence records, mandating expedited public release. Meanwhile, former intelligence officials like Luis Elizondo testified that UAPs have demonstrated capabilities "splitting aircraft formations right down the middle," while retired Rear Admiral Timothy Gallaudet claimed "we've recove [TRUNCATED]

newyorker.dek: The cab-driving Elcharfa brothers, Bilal and Salah, star in a new piece by the artist Kenneth Tam that showcases the hardships of their jobs, Emma Allen writes.
newyorker.text:
On Election Night, Mayor Zohran Mamdani proclaimed, from a stage, “My brother, we are in City Hall now!” The remark was directed at Richard Chow, a cabdriver from Myanmar whom Mamdani had joined in 2021 on a fifteen-day hunger strike, to demand relief for the taxi-medallion debt crisis. The crisis, in brief: the N.Y.C. Taxi and Limousine Commission sells permits (“medallions”) to operate yellow cabs; the medallion—till recently a near-million-dollar investment—had its bottom drop out, owing to predatory lending and the deregulation of rideshare services. The body of Chow’s brother, Kenny, who’d accrued enormous medallion-related debt, was found in the East River in 2018. Chow would later chauffeur Mamdani to Gracie Mansion.
The other day, the artist Kenneth Tam, forty-four, was in Queens, admiring his contribution to MOMA PS1’s “Greater New York” survey of art with ties to the metropolitan area. Title: “I’m Staying Hopeful and Strong (for Bilal and Salah).” A projected video showed the sixtysomething Lebanese taxi-driver brothers Bilal and Salaheldin Elcharfa performing interpretive movements in an empty gymnasium while intoning words they wrote about the crisis (“no retirement no retirement no retirement / scared scared scared”). Also, on the floor of the gallery: wood-beaded seat covers and other car detritus.
Tam had previously worked with nonprofessional performers in his video-art projects (Craigslist-sourced fraternity brothers and cowboys). But recruiting taxi-drivers proved difficult. He tried pitching the idea from back seats, but no luck. Then a black-car driver took him to the “J.F.K. taxi hole.”
“It’s essentially a lot where all the yellow cabs go and take their breaks,” Tam said. Rideshare drivers, too, but they have a separate zone. There’s also a space for prayer. “Ostensibly, they’re there to wait for the call to go to J.F.K., but I met guys that aren’t even drivers anymore, who go to hang out.” Tam and his producer knocked on windows and handed out flyers. Finally, he approached Bilal in the bathroom line. “I did make it clear that this was not necessarily an advocacy project: ‘I’m not an activist, but I do want to tell your story, in a different way.’ ” A way that would eventually involve dancing with metal folding chairs—but one thing at a time.
Outside the museum, Bilal was parked by a fire hydrant in his yellow RAV4. Salah was still en route from Staten Island. Of first meeting Tam, Bilal said, “He asked me, ‘Are you interested in being in a movie?’ ”
“A loose documentary,” Tam clarified.
Bilal was game. “When someone has anger inside them, you want to explore it with the outsider,” he said.
“Did you appreciate then that you would have to dance?” Tam asked.
“It was really interesting, to go for it,” Bilal said. “Falling down, reaching out, maybe looking for some hope. I was sitting in a box where I just felt like, I’m not able to get out of my situation by circling around, making all the moves—this way, that way, left, right, down, up.” He punched a fist into the air.
When things for the brothers turned grim, “we were always leaning on each other,” Bilal said. “Don’t give up. Keep going.” A friend died of a heart attack when the value of his medallion tanked.
“You’re not sick of each other?” Tam asked.
“We fight. But we never really broke up. The next day, it’s as if nothing happened. That’s our attitude.”
Salah called to say that he was stuck on the Verrazzano Bridge.
Tam said that he’d learned to drive in a suburban part of Queens—“and I distinctly remember, for my road test, I didn’t even encounter a traffic light.”
Bilal laughed. “In Lebanon,” he said, “my dad told me, ‘Just drive.’ ”
Salah was still in traffic, so Bilal decided to drive to him. Eventually, he spotted his brother and honked, yelling, “Follow that cab!”
Everyone piled into one car. Salah recounted meeting Tam: “He asked, ‘What kind of song do you like?’ ” The finished video is minimally scored, but, while shooting, Tam had the Elcharfas dance to their favorite tunes. Salah’s: Air Supply’s “Making Love Out of Nothing at All.” Bilal’s: the Scorpions’ “When the Smoke Is Going Down.”
“I’m secretly hoping Mamdani makes an appearance at PS1,” Tam said.
“He’s going to help, but he’s not going to get us back to what was before,” Salah predicted. Both brothers voted for Mamdani, though Bilal also had a laminated photo of Trump (fist raised) dangling from his rearview mirror.
The brothers’ kids recently watched Tam’s film. Bilal: “They were making fun of me—‘You’re getting old! What are you doing?’ ”
Salah: “My daughter, she’s copying me. She starts making the same movements. I said, ‘Maybe I’m going to be a movie star!’ ”
“I explained to them, ‘That’s an art project,’ ” Bilal said. “ ‘The words plus the movement, it makes a story about our situation.’ Hopefully, maybe, we educate the public.” ♦

literary_pick.title: The Immortal Life of Henrietta Lacks
literary_pick.author: Rebecca Skloot
literary_pick.summary: This groundbreaking work of narrative nonfiction tells the story of Henrietta Lacks, a poor Black tobacco farmer whose cancer cells were taken without her knowledge in 1951 and became one of the most important tools in medicine. Known as HeLa cells, they were vital for developing the polio vaccine, cloning, gene mapping, and in vitro fertilization, yet Lacks remained virtually unknown while her family couldn't afford health insurance. The book masterfully weaves together scientific discovery, medical ethics, and the human story of a family grappling with their mother's legacy, becoming a moder [TRUNCATED]

enriched_articles.title: Prince Harry loses legal challenge over security
enriched_articles.text: The Duke of Sussex has lost a legal challenge over the levels of security he and his family are entitled to while in the UK. Prince Harry had been seeking to overturn a decision that had downgraded his security after he stopped being a working royal and moved to the US with the Duchess of Sussex. His lawyers had argued that he had been "singled out" for "inferior treatment", but the court ruling upheld that there had been nothing unlawful about how the decision had been reached.

enriched_articles.title: Prince Harry loses legal challenge over police protection in UK
enriched_articles.text: The Duke of Sussex has lost a legal challenge over the level of taxpayer-funded security he is entitled to while in the UK, allowing the government to proceed with a "bespoke", and cheaper, level of protection for his family. Three senior judges at the court of appeal rejected Prince Harry's claim that he had been "singled out" for "inferior treatment" and that his safety and life were "at stake" after a change in security arrangements that occurred when he stepped down as a working royal and mo

enriched_articles.title: Mass Arrests by Trump's ICE, CBP Repeatedly Fail Under Scrutiny
enriched_articles.text: The National Guard soldiers in desert camo piled out of unmarked vans in East Los Angeles last June, cordoning off East Sixth Street, a residential street lined with single family houses, and blocking a nearby road leading to an elementary school. A squad of federal agents moved in flinging flash-bang grenades — explosives designed to disorient — into a small home before storming inside. enriched_articles.source: The New York Review of Books
enriched_articles.title: In the River of Consciousness | Oliver Sacks
enriched_articles.text: "Time," says Jorge Luis Borges, "is the substance I am made of. Time is a river that carries me away, but I am the river…" Our movements, our actions, are extended in time, as are our perceptions, our thoughts, the contents of consciousness. We live in time, we organize time, we are time creatures through and through. But is the time we live in, or live by, continuous—like Borges's river? Or is it more comparable to a chain or a train, a succession of discrete moments, like beads on a string?

enriched_articles.title: Hallucinations | Oliver Sacks
enriched_articles.text: "To live on a day-to-day basis is insufficient for human beings; we need to transcend, transport, escape; we need meaning, understanding, and explanation; we need to see overall patterns in our lives. We need hope, the sense of a future." —Oliver Sacks Have you ever seen something that wasn't really there? Heard someone call your name in an empty house?
